title: Taiwan
subtitle: 13 days · Clockwise Huandao
body:
Huaxia Cultural Sphere Pt.2: Taiwan

Huandao:
I set out to ride the Huandao, a rite of passage for many Taiwanese. Some walk it, some take motorbikes, and many — foreigners included — complete the loop by bicycle.

I was fortunate to be joined by Nikos, who flew out to ride the island with me. We started in Taipei and rode clockwise, giving ourselves roughly ten days to circumnavigate Taiwan.

The highlight was the descent through Taroko Gorge. It took a day and a half of climbing to reach nearly 9,000 feet, followed by three hours of pure, euphoric descent back to the coast.

The East Coast kept that momentum alive as we raced south, wedged between the restless Pacific Ocean and the still, imposing Hai'an Range. After five days tracing the island's eastern edge, we crossed the Hengchun Peninsula toward the West Coast.

The contrast was immediate. The West felt heavier with dense scooter traffic, constant red lights, and headwinds. Stealth camping became trickier, and the scenery… less scenic.

Flight delays and baggage issues forced Nikos to cut his trip short, leaving me to ride the final day alone. Re-entering Taipei traffic was as frustrating as I remembered, though the city's extensive network of riverside bike paths made slipping back into the center surprisingly easy — the only bit of Taiwanese urban bike infrastructure that works efficiently!

Comparison:
If the mainland is chaotic, direct, and unscripted, Taiwan is relaxed, warm, and predictable.

Ironically, traffic laws in the mainland feel negotiable and flexible. In Taiwan, the rules are tangible. Cross when the light is green. Don't jaywalk. Park where you're told. As a cyclist, I found myself preferring the Chinese driving culture due to the flow, collective vigilance, and the 'dance' you find yourself in.

In the mainland, bureaucracy and compliance are an ever-present backdrop to daily life. Taiwan, by contrast, feels freer at the human scale, largely devoid of an all-reaching system keeping an eye on you. There's necessary order and rules, but I didn't have cops asking for my papers — an experience had in rural Yunnan.

Cycling through China was a notably lonely experience. Taiwan, on the other hand, marked the most social chapter of my entire Asian tour thus far — connections with people felt genuine and authentic. Many new friends were made! Best of all, no staring and people actually say 'Hello!'

This was my second time visiting Taiwan. The island had been my first introduction to East Asia, at the tail end of the COVID era, and the experience played a significant role in deciding on Asia being a destination for a year long bike tour.

The Portuguese referred to the island as 'Formosa' — a title well earned by its beauty and warmth.
